summary:
The Khvalynsk culture was an Eneolithic culture of the middle Volga region, centered on the area around the modern city of Khvalynsk in Saratov Oblast, Russia, dating to approximately 4700–3800 BCE. Along with the [[event:1543|Samara culture]] that preceded it, Khvalynsk represents an early phase in the development of the Pontic-Caspian steppe pastoralist tradition that would culminate in the Yamnaya culture and the spread of [[event:1544|Proto-Indo-European languages]] across Eurasia.

Khvalynsk communities were cattle herders and fishermen occupying the forest-steppe transition zone along the middle Volga. They buried their dead in flat cemeteries (rather than under mounds), with the deceased accompanied by cattle bones as grave offerings — evidence that cattle held significant social and ritual importance. Ochre was frequently scattered in burials, a practice common across the Pontic-Caspian steppe that persisted into later cultures. Horse bones are also present in the Khvalynsk assemblage, suggesting early contact with or adoption of horse herding.

The Khvalynsk culture has attracted scholarly interest as a possible component of the population that eventually gave rise to the Proto-Indo-European speech community. Ancient DNA analysis has confirmed that Khvalynsk-associated individuals carry the genetic profile — including steppe ancestry contributions — that later becomes characteristic of populations associated with PIE language spread. Khvalynsk thus occupies an important position in the developing narrative of Indo-European origins.
